label: Lima
sub: Capital; founded by Pizarro Jan 18, 1535 — center of Viceroyalty, Republic, and all modern political crises

label: Cusco
sub: Inca imperial capital (Tahuantinsuyo); conquered by Pizarro 1533; UNESCO World Heritage City

label: Machu Picchu
sub: Royal estate of Pachacuti, c. 1450; UNESCO World Heritage Site and Peru's top tourist destination (~1.5M visitors/year)

label: Cajamarca
sub: Site of Atahualpa's capture (Nov 16, 1532) — the pivotal moment of the Spanish Conquest; gold ransom room still marked

label: Ayacucho
sub: Birthplace of Shining Path at Univ. Huamanga (1970s); site of 1824 Battle of Ayacucho; hardest-hit department in CVR report

label: Arequipa
sub: Peru's second city; birthplace of multiple presidents; site of 2002 Arequipazo protests against privatization under Toledo

label: Trujillo
sub: APRA stronghold; birthplace of Víctor Raúl Haya de la Torre; site of 1932 APRA uprising and subsequent massacre

label: Chan Chan
sub: Capital of the Chimú Empire (c. 900–1470 CE); largest pre-Columbian city in South America; UNESCO World Heritage Site

label: Lake Titicaca / Puno
sub: Sacred Inca birthplace of the sun; world's highest navigable lake (3,812m); Puno dept site of 18+ deaths in 2023 Boluarte protests

label: Iquitos
sub: World's largest city unreachable by road; rubber boom capital 1880–1912; gateway to the Peruvian Amazon

label: Chuschi
sub: Site of Shining Path's first attack — ballot boxes burned on May 17, 1980, on the eve of Peru's first universal suffrage election

label: Tarata Street, Miraflores
sub: Shining Path car bomb, July 16, 1992: 25 killed, 155 wounded — deadliest terrorist attack in Lima's history

label: La Cantuta / Cieneguilla
sub: Grupo Colina abducted 9 students + 1 professor from La Cantuta University (July 18, 1992); remains found at Cieneguilla burial site

label: Uchuraccay
sub: Massacre of 8 journalists by terrified villagers, Jan 26, 1983; Mario Vargas Llosa led government commission into its causes

label: Junín Plateau
sub: Battle of Junín, August 6, 1824 — Bolívar's cavalry defeats royalists at 4,100m; decisive battle leading to Ayacucho

label: Pampa de Quinua
sub: Battle of Ayacucho, December 9, 1824 — Sucre defeats last Spanish viceroy; final battle of South American independence

label: Nazca Lines
sub: Geoglyphs of the Nazca culture (100 BCE – 800 CE); UNESCO World Heritage Site; over 1,000 km of lines covering 450 km²

label: Callao
sub: Peru's main port; Royal Callao fortified under Viceroyalty; San Martín's liberation fleet landed nearby; current narco-trafficking hub

label: Tingo María
sub: Gateway to the VRAEM (Apurímac, Ene, Mantaro River Valley) — Peru's coca and residual Shining Path zone; major drug trafficking hub

label: Puno City
sub: Epicenter of Boluarte protest violence (Jan 9, 2023): 18 killed in single week; Aymara indigenous population at forefront of anti-government uprising

label: Huancayo
sub: Central Andes commercial hub; strategic base for La Breña resistance campaign by Cáceres against Chilean occupation (1881–1884)

label: Vilcabamba
sub: Last refuge of Neo-Inca state (1536–1572); Manco Inca's jungle stronghold; Tupac Amaru I captured here and taken to Cusco for execution

label: Bagua
sub: Baguazo, June 5, 2009: police clash with Awajún-Wampís indigenous protesters blocking Amazon road; 33 indigenous + 24 police killed

label: Japanese Ambassador's Residence
sub: MRTA seized 72 hostages Dec 17, 1996; Operation Chavín de Huántar rescue, April 22, 1997: all 14 MRTA killed, 72 hostages freed

label: Congreso de la República
sub: Scene of all six executive-legislative crises 2016–2022; Odebrecht hearings; impeachments of Kuczynski, Vizcarra, and Castillo

label: Lord of Sipán Tomb
sub: Moche royal tomb discovered April 1987 near Chiclayo by archaeologist Walter Alva — the 'Tutankhamun of the Americas'; unlooted 3rd-century CE burial with gold funerary mask, warrior-priest artifacts, and full sacrificial entourage

label: Wari (Huari) Capital
sub: Capital of the Wari Empire (600–1100 CE), 25 km north of Ayacucho — first pan-Andean imperial state, predecessor to the Inca; city housed 35,000–70,000 at its peak; collapse poorly understood

label: Pisac (Sacred Valley)
sub: Inca royal estate and terracing complex in the Urubamba Valley; agricultural terraces (andenes) fed thousands; hilltop citadel and Sunday market survive as major Andean cultural landmarks

label: Ollantaytambo
sub: Site of the only Inca military victory over Pizarro (January 1536) — Manco Inca repelled Spanish cavalry from massive stone terraces; best-preserved living Inca town; used as launch point for Vilcabamba retreat

label: Tacna
sub: Captured by Chile May 1880; administered as Chilean territory for 49 years; returned to Peru June 28, 1929 after referendum — last War of the Pacific territorial settlement; known as 'La Ciudad Heroica' for its resistance

label: Barrios Altos Massacre Site
sub: November 3, 1991: Grupo Colina death squad burst into a neighborhood party, killing 15 civilians including an 8-year-old; Inter-American Court of Human Rights landmark ruling (2001) found Peru responsible for crimes against humanity ordered by Fujimori

label: Los Cabitos Military Base
sub: Ayacucho army base used as clandestine detention and torture center 1983–1995; mass graves with 109+ victims discovered 2005–2012; key CVR evidentiary site; forensic exhumations ongoing as of 2026

label: Piura
sub: Peru's fifth-largest city; founded January 1532 — Spain's first permanent settlement in South America; northern coast agricultural hub; repeatedly devastated by El Niño flooding (1983, 1998, 2017); APRA political base

label: Huaraz / Ancash Earthquake
sub: May 31, 1970: 7.9 Mw earthquake triggered Mt. Huascarán avalanche that buried Yungay and Ranrahirca; 66,000–70,000 killed — worst natural disaster in Western Hemisphere in the 20th century; accelerated Velasco's military reforms

label: Andahuaylazo (Andahuaylas)
sub: January 1, 2005: Antauro Humala (Ollanta's brother) led ethnocacerista reservists in armed takeover of police stations, killing 4 officers; demanded Toledo's resignation; Antauro imprisoned 2005–2022, pardoned by Castillo

label: Puerto Maldonado
sub: Capital of Madre de Dios region; gateway to Amazonian biodiversity (Manu National Park); epicenter of illegal gold mining crisis; Pope Francis met Amazonian indigenous communities here January 19, 2018, highlighting deforestation and extractivism

label: El Frontón Prison Island
sub: Island prison off Callao; June 18–19, 1986: military assault on Shining Path inmates after riot — CVR found at least 111 surrendered prisoners executed; one of three concurrent 'prison massacres' ordered under President Alan García's government
